pg107-p1-s2:
As animals on a treadmill, we have patiently and wearily climbed, falling back
in exhaustion after each futile effort to reach solid ground.
pg107-p1-s3:
Most of us have entered the final stage with its
commitment to health resorts, sanitariums, hospitals, and jails.
pg107-p1-s4: Sometimes there were screaming delirium and insanity.
pg107-p1:
these things terrified and distracted us. As animals on a treadmill, we have patiently and wearily climbed, falling back
in exhaustion after each futile effort to reach solid ground. Most of us have entered the final stage with its
commitment to health resorts, sanitariums, hospitals, and jails. Sometimes there were screaming delirium and insanity. Death was often near.
pg107-p2-s1: Under these conditions we naturally made mistakes.
pg107-p2-s2: Some of them rose out of ignorance of alcoholism.
pg107-p2-s3:
Sometimes we sensed
dimly that we were dealing with sick men.
pg107-p2-s4:
Had we fully understood the nature of the alcoholic illness, we might have
behaved differently.
pg107-p2:
Under these conditions we naturally made mistakes. Some of them rose out of ignorance of alcoholism. Sometimes we sensed
dimly that we were dealing with sick men. Had we fully understood the nature of the alcoholic illness, we might have
behaved differently.
pg107-p3-s1: How could men who loved their wives and children be so unthinking, so callous, so cruel?
pg107-p3-s2:
There could be no love in such
persons, we thought.
pg107-p3-s3:
And just as we were being convinced of their heartlessness, they would surprise us with fresh
resolves and new attentions.
pg107-p3-s4:
For a while they would be their old sweet selves, only to dash the new structure of
affection to pieces once more.
pg107-p3-s5:
Asked why they commenced to drink again, they would reply with some silly excuse, or
none.
pg107-p3-s6: It was so baffling, so heartbreaking.
pg107-p3-s7: Could we have been so mistaken in the men we married?
pg107-p3-s9: Sometimes they were so inaccessible that it seemed as though a great wall had been built around them.
pg107-p3:
How could men who loved their wives and children be so unthinking, so callous, so cruel? There could be no love in such
persons, we thought. And just as we were being convinced of their heartlessness, they would surprise us with fresh
resolves and new attentions. For a while they would be their old sweet selves, only to dash the new structure of
affection to pieces once more. Asked why they commenced to drink again, they would reply with some silly excuse, or
none. It was so baffling, so heartbreaking. Could we have been so mistaken in the men we married? When drinking, they
were strangers. Sometimes they were so inaccessible that it seemed as though a great wall had been built around them.
pg107-p4-s1: And even if they did not love their families, how could they be so blind about themselves?
pg107-p4-s2:
What had become of their
judgment, their common sense, their will power?
pg107-p4-s3: Why could they not see that drink meant ruin to them?
pg107-p4-s4:
Why was it, when
these dangers were
